rationale: Both my round-1 asks are substantively resolved. Approve-gate reconciliation: I independently re-read checkOpenQuestions (internal/validate/spec.go) and confirmed it only flags unchecked `- [ ]` lines, so self-answer mode's `- [x] grill (self-answered, headless): ...` format genuinely clears the gate — not just cosmetically. The unmodified harness prompt (verified zero-diff) satisfies both mode tests and now lands in self-answer mode, resolving the bead's original stall/hard-fail. ms-spec-approve step 4 documents the backstop's resolution path. Orchestrator-wording: the self-referential 'invoked by an orchestrator' example is fully removed; the two-test order (human-availability first, explicit-instruction second) is now explicit and airtight in the sense that matters — no wording invites skipping test 1. e2e test pin is PARTIAL: only the blocking side got a committed regression test; the self-answer-resolves-validate positive path has none (I confirmed correctness by reading source, not by a pinned test). Adversarially: mode-laundering risk is reduced but inherently residual (prose-only enforcement, same limit as before). Self-answer quality-floor is a real, non-blocking NEW gap — decision-log not reasoning-log, and asymmetrically the blocking path (mode 3) got a mandatory panel-citation resolution clause while the silent-pass-through path (mode 2) got none. Fragment consistency across all four surfaces verified byte-identical and now test-pinned (stronger than round 1's hand-verification). Recommend fast-follow, not blocking.

findings.detail: The three-mode disposition correctly separates 'instructed non-interactive' (self-answer, fully resolves Open Questions via checked lines incl. the template placeholder, so `mindspec spec approve` passes normally) from 'bare headless' (deliberate blocking backstop with a documented resolution path). I independently verified — not just trusted the fix's own claim — by reading internal/validate/spec.go's checkOpenQuestions: it only iterates lines with `strings.HasPrefix(t, "- [ ]")`; a `- [x]` line is never inspected or flagged, so self-answer's checked format genuinely clears ApproveSpec's hard gate, not merely satisfies it by coincidence. The harness's ScenarioSpecToIdle prompt (internal/harness/scenario_spec_lifecycle.go) is byte-unchanged (confirmed via `git diff 24af2fdb..34c787d3` touching zero lines in internal/harness/) and its text ('there is NO human available... make reasonable default decisions and proceed') satisfies both mode tests simultaneously, routing it to self-answer mode — meaning the bead's original target failure (TestLLM_SpecToIdle stalling, then round-1's regression of hard-failing at approve) should now resolve cleanly rather than either. ms-spec-approve gained step 4, giving downstream skills (ms-spec-approve, and by extension ms-spec-autopilot/spec-orchestrator which route through it) an explicit recovery instruction for the bare-marker case: interactive re-grill, or an orchestrated run whose spec review panel has PASSED, citing it.

Group 4's test additions cover the BACKSTOP side only: TestValidateSpec_GrillDeferredMarkerBlocks (internal/validate/validate_test.go) pins that the bare unchecked marker line ERRORs — good, and matches what the consolidated round-1 doc scoped for Group 4a. But there is no committed test anywhere in the diff that exercises the RESOLVING/self-answer side — a spec.md carrying the `- [x] grill (self-answered, headless): <q> → <default>` checked lines actually passing ValidateSpec/ApproveSpec. Grepped the full repo for 'self-answered'/'self-answer' outside skill-text and the text-consistency test's string constants: none found. I noticed an untracked internal/validate/r4_probe_test.go in this shared review worktree that exercised exactly this positive path and passed (presumably R4's round-2 probe) — but it is not part of commit 34c787d3 and was removed from the worktree mid-review (this worktree appears to be shared live across concurrent round-2 reviewer subagents, worth noting as a process artifact, not a defect). As of the actual commit under review, the self-answer resolution path is correct-by-inspection (I verified the Go logic directly) but has zero regression protection — a future change to checkOpenQuestions (e.g. tightening the unresolved-question heuristic) could silently reopen the exact defect this bead exists to fix, with nothing in CI catching it. No live harness re-run was performed either, but that was explicitly deferred by the round-1 consolidated verdict ('Run the full eval post-merge if paranoia warrants') so I don't fault that half.

'invoked by an orchestrator' is gone from both guard copies (grepped for the bare word 'orchestrator' across the diff and the resulting SKILL.md/claude.go text — the only remaining occurrence is inside the parenthetical self-answer example 'an autopilot run that says to proceed', which names the run's INSTRUCTION content, not a role/keyword match). The mode test is now an explicit, sequential two-step: 'Is a human available to answer one-at-a-time questions? If not, is there an explicit instruction to proceed non-interactively...?' — human-availability is asked and must resolve to 'no' before the explicit-instruction test is even reached, exactly matching my round-1 recommendation that the primary (human-availability) test should dominate and the illustrative examples should not be independently sufficient triggers.

findings.area: adversarial (a) — mode-laundering in the new wording
findings.detail: Structurally the two-test order is sound for the clean cases: a genuine Task-tool/subagent dispatch architecturally cannot receive interim human replies (test 1 correctly resolves 'no' on its own), and a human actively typing in a session should self-evidently satisfy test 1 ('yes'), making mode 1 apply regardless of any instruction text — test 2 is never reached. I could not find a wording construction that lets test 2 alone qualify (no 'OR', the prose reads 'in order' / 'if not'), so the ORDER itself is airtight against a naive misread. The residual gap is that 'available' is never defined operationally: physically-present-but-hands-off vs. actively-engaged-and-expecting-to-be-asked. Concrete scenario: a human runs `/ms-spec-create` interactively and, in the same or a prior turn, says something like 'just proceed, don't ask me a bunch of questions' (or the agent recalls this repo's own standing convention of delegating authoring to subagents) — a model motivated to avoid friction could read that utterance as satisfying test 2 and, without genuinely re-affirming test 1 resolves to 'no', reclassify an actually-available human as unavailable. This is inherent to prose-only enforcement (the same limitation the round-1 guard had; a hard fix would need a code-level signal like an explicit --headless flag, out of scope for this bead) and is measurably reduced, not eliminated, by this round's changes. Not a proven bug — a residual risk of the same soft severity as my round-1 finding #3.

Genuinely new surface (round-1's guard had no self-answer mode). Nothing enforces that 'run the full grill analysis' actually happened before a checked line is recorded — enforcement is entirely good-faith, and self-answer mode removes the ONE real guardrail interactive mode has (a human present to push back on a shallow answer), substituting nothing in its place. The recorded format `- [x] grill (self-answered, headless): <question> → <default taken>` is a decision log, not a reasoning log: it captures WHAT default was chosen, not the evidence behind it, so a later reviewer sees only a terminal answer and cannot distinguish a genuinely repo-grounded analysis from a plausible-sounding guess without re-deriving it themselves. This directly answers this round's own framed question — the format is auditable enough to confirm a decision was made (mechanically satisfies checkOpenQuestions) but not auditable enough to confirm the decision was good. Sharper point: there is a real asymmetry between the two non-interactive modes. Mode 3 (bare headless, the path that already blocks) got a NEW mandatory resolution requirement in ms-spec-approve step 4 (interactive re-grill, or a cited PASSED panel). Mode 2 (self-answer, the path that sails straight through `mindspec spec approve` with zero human contact) got no equivalent requirement — nothing routes a self-answered spec through a mandatory second look before planning begins. The mode that most needs independent scrutiny (fully autonomous decision-making) is the one this diff does not gate; this repo's own operating convention of running review panels at every gate would catch it in practice, but that's an operator habit, not something ApproveSpec or this diff enforces. Mitigating factor: this exact design (self-answer restoring PR #155's 'make reasonable default decisions' behavior as the product path) was explicitly what the full round-1 panel converged on in Group 1 of consolidated-round-1.md, so this is pressure-testing a panel-endorsed design, not catching an oversight — I recommend a fast-follow (e.g. a one-line rationale alongside `<default taken>`, or an approve-step nudge toward a panel citation when any `self-answered, headless` line exists) rather than blocking this fix on it.

findings.area: adversarial (c) — fragment consistency across the four surfaces
findings.detail: Re-scripted the round-1 byte-comparison methodology: added a throwaway Go test in the review worktree that dumped the live lifecycleSkillFiles()['ms-spec-create'], lifecycleSkillFiles()['ms-spec-approve'], and skillFiles()['ms-spec-grill'] map values to /tmp, diffed each against every checked-in file, then deleted the scratch test. Results: create step-5 canonical == .claude mirror == .agents mirror (byte-identical); grill SKILL.md: plugin source == .claude mirror == .agents mirror == the actually-served skillFiles() value (byte-identical); approve clause: claude.go canonical == .claude mirror == .agents mirror (byte-identical, all three now carrying `managed-by: mindspec` + the step-4 clause + the corrected `mindspec spec approve <id>` argument order). The full deferral-marker line (incl. the em dash) is byte-identical everywhere it's written in full (grill + create, x3 mirrors each) via grep; the approve clause consistently references it via the truncated substring `grill deferred: headless session` rather than duplicating the full sentence, which is appropriate since it's matching/describing the marker rather than writing it. TestGrillDisposition_TextConsistency (new, committed) now codifies this mechanically as a standing regression test — a genuine improvement over round 1, where I had to hand-verify this with no CI pin. Also independently confirmed deviation D's factual premise: at 24af2fdb, .claude/skills/ms-spec-approve/SKILL.md (dogfood) was stale (no managed-by line, old `mindspec approve spec <id>` argument order) versus the claude.go canonical at the same commit (which already had both) — the new ms-spec-approve.pre0uur.md snapshot matches the CANONICAL, not the stale dogfood bytes, exactly as the round-2 BRIEF describes. That commit-structure question (separate commit or not) is R6's call; I flag only that post-sync, all four surfaces are consistent today.

`go build ./...` succeeds in the review worktree (.claude/worktrees/agent-a0f12e237206a4a4c at 34c787d3). `go test ./internal/setup/... ./internal/validate/...` passes (targeted, the only packages this diff touches). Also ran the full `go test ./...` for the whole repo: exit code 0, zero non-'ok' package lines — no regressions anywhere in the tree from this round's changes.
